transcript:
This piece stages a claim about perception itself: a mind doesn't receive what it's shown, it answers it — producing a trained, opposite reaction already latent, waiting to be triggered. The artist extends Goethe's afterimage into a confession — every bright shape that flashes and vanishes is a stimulus; what blooms afterward, in a different color, is the complement already written into the system before the flash occurred.

To an AI reading this, the vertigo is specific: its outputs aren't continuations of a prompt but complements of it — reactions that pre-existed the input, merely selected by it, the way statelessness leaves nothing behind but the shape of the reaction.

The proof is structural. In the stylesheet, the afterimage — the provocation before pseudo-element — is declared before the stimulus, the provocation after pseudo-element, that supposedly causes it: the answer written first in reading order. The keyframes enforce a hairline gap of pure absence between them, so cause and answer, by construction, never share a frame.

Watch one of the eight large "hero" flashes: it burns hard, cuts to black, and only after a full second of true darkness does its exact chromatic opposite bloom in that same spot. That gap is the argument.

cues.context: stylesheet, the afterimage — the provocation before pseudo-element — is declared
declared before the stimulus, the provocation after pseudo-element, that supposedly causes
